content: Use caveman ultra skills for token efficiency. For this exercise act as a principal cloud engineer with strong Kubernetes operator datacenter systems and control plane design experience: 1st read [sknrao/opi-assignment-1-2026](https://github.com/sknrao/opi-assignment-1-2026) then inspect the actual OPI DPU operator and NVIDIA DPF codebases before proposing anything. Think analyze and tell me the answer in a detailed but technical way also too.

Me read repos first. OPI seam files: `internal/platform/vendordetector.go`, `internal/controller/dataprocessingunit_controller.go`, `dpu-api/api.proto`, `api/v1/dataprocessingunit_types.go`, `api/v1/dataprocessingunitconfig_types.go`, `api/v1/dpuoperatorconfig_types.go`. NVIDIA side: `DPUDiscovery`, `DPUFlavor`, `DPUSet`, `DPUServiceNAD`, `ServiceInterface`, `DPUService`, `DPUServiceChain`, `DPFOperatorConfig`. Good base.

Okay. Also search these and collect the real links for me because I want source grounded writing: [vendordetector.go](https://github.com/opiproject/dpu-operator/blob/main/internal/platform/vendordetector.go) [dataprocessingunit_controller.go](https://github.com/opiproject/dpu-operator/blob/main/internal/controller/dataprocessingunit_controller.go) [api.proto](https://github.com/opiproject/dpu-operator/blob/main/dpu-api/api.proto) [DPF overview](https://docs.nvidia.com/networking/display/dpf25101) [system overview](https://github.com/NVIDIA/doca-platform/blob/public-main/docs/public/developer-guides/architecture/system-overview.md) and [component description](https://github.com/NVIDIA/doca-platform/blob/public-main/docs/public/developer-guides/architecture/component-description.md).

Me grouped refs. OPI seam refs: `vendordetector.go`, `dataprocessingunit_controller.go`, `dpu-api/api.proto`. NVIDIA refs: DPF overview, system overview, component description. Enough to ground boundary 1st.

Good, also follow my writing directions from the start: all headings should end with colons and all points should end with periods. Use simple technical English concise academic style and less fluff. Search more refs too for `DataProcessingUnit`, `DataProcessingUnitConfig`, and `DpuOperatorConfig` and tell me if they matter.

These OPI refs matter. `api/v1/dataprocessingunit_types.go` = per DPU state. `api/v1/dataprocessingunitconfig_types.go` = config surface still scaffold. `api/v1/dpuoperatorconfig_types.go` = operator config still narrow. This pushes phase 1 toward smaller mapping.
Okay. Do not just give vibe: tell me what the OPI code actually implies about the current extension seam.

OPI already has vendor seam. Detector manager registers vendor. `dpu-api/api.proto` defines VSP gRPC. `DataProcessingUnitReconciler` deploys vendor pods. Active RPCs: `Init`, `GetDevices`, `SetNumVfs`, `CreateNetworkFunction`, `DeleteNetworkFunction`, `Ping`.
Inspect NVIDIA DPF with that same discipline: is it a thin helper library, or is it already its own operator domain.
DPF not helper lib. DPF = real operator domain: provisioning, service plane, host cluster + DPU cluster split. API much richer than OPI CRDs. Pulling it into OPI would duplicate control plane.

Now think and analyze this: I want the narrowest boundary that keeps OPI vendor neutral maximizes DPF reuse and still looks implementable. Give me detailed options 1st then tell me which option you think is strongest.

3 options. 1: absorb DPF logic into OPI. Bad, dup control plane. 2: thin NVIDIA adapter behind VSP seam. Best, OPI stays neutral, adapter translates, DPF owns BlueField lifecycle. 3: direct high level DPF CRD translation from current OPI API. Too early. Pick 2.

Okay. Option 2 seems good so let us choose that for now. But before we lock it in also too pressure test the attractive alternative: should phase 1 translate directly into a high level DPF object like `DPUDeployment` or is that too optimistic for current OPI.
Direct `DPUDeployment` now = too optimistic. `DpuOperatorConfig` narrow. `DataProcessingUnitConfig` scaffold. NF input endpoint oriented. High level translation would overstate present OPI intent.

Okay. Since we are choosing the thin adapter path map the phase 1 path carefully: what should `Init`, `SetNumVfs`, and `CreateNetworkFunction` become on the DPF side. Think analyze and tell me in a detailed way.

`Init` -> check DPF discovery, create or patch `DPUFlavor`, create or patch `DPUSet`, let DPF realize downstream. `SetNumVfs` -> stay declarative through provisioning objs. `CreateNetworkFunction` -> start at `DPUServiceNAD` + `ServiceInterface`; add `DPUService` or `DPUServiceChain` only when intent truly needs them.
I order you to check the current OPI service request shape too, not just the CRDs: are we even given enough information to justify broad service side translation.
Not enough yet. I checked current path, incl `internal/daemon/dpusidemanager.go`. Service input still endpoint pair oriented. So phase 1 should stay at `DPUServiceNAD` + `ServiceInterface`.
Find and dentify small but important API gaps on the OPI side: if there is one additive field that materially improves the design call it out.
Best additive OPI fix: store raw detector identity in `DataProcessingUnit.spec.dpuIdentifier`. Makes `Init(dpu_identifier)` lossless. No name rebuild hack later.

Okay. For the bonus code do not give me toy pseudocode. Think through the options and tell me. We can do a simple self contained mock a half realistic custom scaffold or something closer to a real controller. I want something that feels like a foundational operator implementation even if it is still an assignment deliverable.

3 code shapes. 1: tiny mock. Compiles, not convincing. 2: custom scaffold. Better, still fake. 3: real `controller-runtime` slice. Best. Use watched `DataProcessingUnit`, real DPF GVKs, unstructured apply, separate status reconciler.
Let we choose Option 3 for the code skeleton. Now status is where these designs usually get vague: I want a clear ownership model and a clear condition model.

Ownership split strict. OPI writes only `DataProcessingUnit.status.conditions`. Adapter writes only owned DPF specs. DPF controllers write DPF status. Adapter labels owned objs, watches them, maps back to DPU, surfaces `TranslationValid`, `Progressing`, `Degraded`, `Ready`.
So...for the write up, I need this to read like a technical design document, not like a brainstorm: what diagrams and structure should `architecture_design.md` have.

`architecture_design.md` should have: problem statement, codebase grounding, constraints, chosen arch, sequence diagrams, ownership + status flow, trade offs, validation + limits, future vendor onboarding plan. Mermaid inline. PNG renders in `diagrams/`.
Okay. System diagrams get too long very quickly: if Figure 1 becomes too wide split it cleanly.
Split Figure 1. Figure 1A = control plane path OPI -> adapter -> DPF -> BlueField side. Figure 1B = responsibility split + status return path.
So....be careful about scope: the assignment explicitly values approach over fake completeness. Reflect that in both the document and the code.
Need explicit boundaries. Doc must say what validated, what deferred, why phase 1 stops at thin adapter + lower level DPF translation. Code must show real reconcile seam, not fake end to end proof.

Well..static reading is not enough for me: think and analyze the validation path too. What validation should we run so this is technically credible and what are the realistic options if we want more than just compile level confidence.

3 validation levels. 1: doc syntax only. Too weak. 2: Linux compile + `go test ./...` + `go vet ./...` + JSON + Mermaid checks. Required. 3: live K8s with real OPI + DPF CRDs. Best extra proof. Pick 2 + 3.
Good, let us choose Option 2 plus Option 3. But here is one practical problem: WSL is awkward for parts of this. Think analyze and tell me the better Linux validation path if we get stuck there.
2 paths. Fight WSL, keep env noise. Or move live validation to real Linux VM. Pick VM. Ubuntu in VirtualBox + small `k3s` cluster = cleaner control plane env, enough for assignment proof.

Okay. I chose the Ubuntu VM plus `k3s` path and ran it. Now we are stuck on the 1st real issue: raw product strings produced invalid Kubernetes label values. Analyze that and update the design and skeleton accordingly.
Good live finding. Fix by sanitizing generated label values before write. Doc should say live validation exposed this. Makes impl + validation story stronger.
2nd real issue from live testing: `DPUFlavor.spec` turned out to be immutable. We were effectively stuck there too. Think, analyze, and tell me the correct phase 1 way to handle VF count changes.
Fix: stop mutating existing flavor. `SetNumVfs` should create new `DPUFlavor` revision for target VF count, then repoint owning `DPUSet` to new flavor.

Well..there is one more mismatch between the ideal design and upstream reality: current OPI does not yet carry `spec.dpuIdentifier` but our architecture wants it. So....analyze the options and tell me how to bridge that without lying in the final deliverables.
Model `spec.dpuIdentifier` as target additive API fix. Keep fallback for current upstream: spec field 1st, then `opi.dpu/raw-identifier` annotation, then name fallback. Honest and practical.
So....tighten the service side wording too: I do not want phase 1 to overclaim anything beyond what the current OPI input shape can honestly support.

Service section should say phase 1 stops at `DPUServiceNAD` + `ServiceInterface` because OPI NF input still endpoint pair oriented. `DPUService` + `DPUServiceChain` come later if OPI grows richer declarative model.
Okay. I also want a useful vendor generalization section: not vague extensibility language but a real future vendor onboarding plan.

Vendor onboarding plan should be procedural. Confirm OPI seam. Read vendor stack at source level. Add only minimal OPI identity fields. Keep same adapter boundary. Validate early against live CRDs so immutable fields, selectors, label rules surface fast.

Okay. Search more refs too because I want a lot of direct links in the final document: [dataprocessingunit_types.go](https://github.com/opiproject/dpu-operator/blob/main/api/v1/dataprocessingunit_types.go) [dpuoperatorconfig_types.go](https://github.com/opiproject/dpu-operator/blob/main/api/v1/dpuoperatorconfig_types.go) [dpuflavor_types.go](https://github.com/NVIDIA/doca-platform/blob/public-main/api/provisioning/v1alpha1/dpuflavor_types.go) [dpuset_types.go](https://github.com/NVIDIA/doca-platform/blob/public-main/api/provisioning/v1alpha1/dpuset_types.go) [dpudiscovery_types.go](https://github.com/NVIDIA/doca-platform/blob/public-main/api/provisioning/v1alpha1/dpudiscovery_types.go). Analyze them and tell me what each one gives us.

`dataprocessingunit_types.go` shows current per device OPI state and why `dpuIdentifier` helps. `dpuoperatorconfig_types.go` shows operator intent still limited. `dpudiscovery_types.go` grounds `Init` discovery step. `dpuflavor_types.go` grounds VF/NIC config. `dpuset_types.go` grounds provisioning attachment point. Core refs for phase 1 provisioning.

Good, also search these service side refs and tell me how they map to our claims: [dpuservicenad_types.go](https://github.com/NVIDIA/doca-platform/blob/public-main/api/dpuservice/v1alpha1/dpuservicenad_types.go) [serviceinterface_types.go](https://github.com/NVIDIA/doca-platform/blob/public-main/api/dpuservice/v1alpha1/serviceinterface_types.go) [dpuservice_types.go](https://github.com/NVIDIA/doca-platform/blob/public-main/api/dpuservice/v1alpha1/dpuservice_types.go) [dpuservicechain_types.go](https://github.com/NVIDIA/doca-platform/blob/public-main/api/dpuservice/v1alpha1/dpuservicechain_types.go) [dpudeployment_types.go](https://github.com/NVIDIA/doca-platform/blob/public-main/api/dpuservice/v1alpha1/dpudeployment_types.go) [dpusidemanager.go](https://github.com/opiproject/dpu-operator/blob/main/internal/daemon/dpusidemanager.go) and [opi-nvidia-bridge](https://github.com/opiproject/opi-nvidia-bridge).

Service refs support scope choices. `dpuservicenad_types.go` + `serviceinterface_types.go` support narrow phase 1 attachment model. `dpuservice_types.go` + `dpuservicechain_types.go` show richer orchestration later. `dpudeployment_types.go` shows tempting but premature high level path. `internal/daemon/dpusidemanager.go` proves endpoint oriented runtime input. `opi-nvidia-bridge` shows adapter precedent in OPI ecosystem.
So....I want the document to show exactly where the design is grounded: list the OPI and DPF files that matter most and use them as references.

Main refs. OPI: `internal/platform/vendordetector.go`, `internal/controller/dataprocessingunit_controller.go`, `dpu-api/api.proto`, `api/v1/dataprocessingunit_types.go`, `api/v1/dataprocessingunitconfig_types.go`, `api/v1/dpuoperatorconfig_types.go`. DPF: `DPFOperatorConfig`, `DPUDiscovery`, `DPUFlavor`, `DPUSet`, `DPUServiceNAD`, `ServiceInterface`, `DPUService`, `DPUServiceChain`, `DPUDeployment`, plus DPF arch docs.

Well....I am still not fully satisfied with the code skeleton. A standard library mock would compile yes but it would not look implementation ready. Since we chose the more realistic option tell me in detail what changed in the code once we decided to fix that.

Code moved from pseudo scaffold to real K8s controller shape. Now uses `controller-runtime`, real DPF GVKs via unstructured objs, `builder`, `handler`, `predicate`, secondary watches, apply style ownership, separate `DPUStatusReconciler`. Feels like real operator slice now.

So...the transcript itself should show where you were stuck and how that got fixed. Right now it still feels too smooth. Think, analyze, and tell me the actual stuck points we should preserve, and how each one was resolved.

Stuck points. 1: `DPUDeployment` looked elegant, but API too rich for current OPI, so we chose thinner adapter + lower level DPF objs. 2: tiny self contained skeleton would compile, but not look implementation ready, so we chose `controller-runtime`. 3: live validation exposed bad label values + immutable `DPUFlavor.spec`; both needed direct fixes. 4: `spec.dpuIdentifier` missing upstream, so target model + fallback path.
Good, keep those and add the environment side too: we were also stuck on proving things in an actual Linux validation path then moved to the Ubuntu VM plus `k3s` route.
Keep env struggle too. Transcript should show Linux validation path awkward at 1st, then moved to Ubuntu VM + small `k3s` CRD harness. Good proof path, not vague future work.
Also be explicit about limits: do not let the final files imply that we validated a full real DPF deployment with actual BlueField hardware if we did not.
Limits must stay explicit. We proved compile, tests, and live CRD harness behavior. We did not claim full end to end DPF reconcile on real BlueField hardware.
Okay. I want the status section to read like operator engineering not like just vibe: give me the condition vocabulary and retry behavior cleanly.

`TranslationValid` = can map current OPI input to legal DPF objs. `Progressing` = owned objs missing or still reconciling. `Degraded` = recoverable vendor side failure. `Ready` = required provisioning + service objs present and healthy. Progress keeps retrying. Translation mismatch blocks. Vendor failures stay visible while watches continue.
So...add the watch model too: I want it clear that status is event driven and not just based on blind polling.
Status path should be event driven. Watch owned `DPUFlavor`, `DPUSet`, `DPUServiceNAD`, `ServiceInterface`. Map events back to owning `DataProcessingUnit`. Keep slow requeue only as safety net.
Well...if OPI later grows a richer declarative model I do not want the document to sound like phase 1 blocks that future: say how `DPUDeployment` fits later.
Doc should say lower level translation is honest phase 1, not hard ceiling. If OPI later grows richer declarative intent, same adapter boundary can move upward toward `DPUDeployment`.
So....in the diagrams, keep the host side and BlueField execution domains visible. That split is important to the story.
Yes. Figure 1A should keep OPI host domain, NVIDIA DPF host domain, BlueField execution domain visible. This makes control plane + execution split obvious.
Fine. 1 more document quality issue: avoid meta sounding lines and make the writing feel more like a concise academic technical document.
Writeup should stay direct, evidence based, concise. Cut meta lines. Cut filler. Let source grounding + diagrams carry argument.
Include the actual validation story too: local compile, Linux checks, and live cluster checks against actual CRDs, plus the Oracle VirtualBox Ubuntu note in a concise way.

Validation section should say `feature_skeleton.go` compiled on Linux with real K8s + `controller-runtime` deps, checked with `go test` + `go vet`, then exercised in live `k3s` harness against actual OPI + DPF CRDs inside Ubuntu on VirtualBox. Also state exact limits.

Good, do one more document refinement pass too: headings should end with colons points should end with periods and the references should support the exact claims near them. Also keep the diagrams technically accurate.

Good final doc pass should tighten structure + evidence. Keep headings with colons. Keep point endings with periods. Make nearby refs support nearby claims. Keep diagrams accurate to control plane, ownership, status, provisioning, service flow.

So....now test the bonus deliverable again after the live fixes: I want the Linux compile path the focused checks and the live CRD harness story to stay correct. Tell me exactly what that proves and what it does not.

Re test should prove 3 things. `feature_skeleton.go` still builds with real K8s + `controller-runtime` deps. Focused checks like `go test` + `go vet` still pass. Live `k3s` CRD harness still accepts object shapes after label sanitization + flavor revision fix. It does not prove full DPF reconcile on real BlueField hardware.
Okay. Do a final consistency pass across `architecture_design.md` `feature_skeleton.go` and `llm_transcript.json`: scope references validation claims and design choices should all line up.

Final consistency pass should confirm same story in all 3 files. `architecture_design.md` explains thin adapter boundary, lower level phase 1 mapping, refs, diagrams, limits. `feature_skeleton.go` implements same shape with watches, conditions, fallback identity path, label sanitization, flavor revisioning. `llm_transcript.json` should now show same decisions, same stuck points, same fixes, same validation scope.
